$comment: SMGP team-season history capsules, season 5 (SMGP-024). Base-universe history: the world's own arc, NOT the player career. DISPLAY-ONLY, never a fold input.

team.madonna.summary: One test was all the king needed. While every other garage spent the spring coaxing its new monster past half-distance, A. Senna absorbed the most violent regulation change in the circus's history in a single session and posted a benchmark nobody approached all season. The MADONNA 456 took the Gen-4 thunder like a tailor's new cloth, the PALM 190 V10 re-armed and still incapable of raising its voice, and A. Asselin resumed the door without a word said either way. The smoke plague wrote everyone else's story. The house read it, from the front, with mild interest.
team.madonna.objective: Bend the new formula to the benchmark before anyone else has read it twice.
team.madonna.definingEvent: The single pre-season test: one session, one benchmark, a spring of futile chasing.
team.madonna.carryForward: The cathedrals belong to steel for now, and the crown files the smoke plague under other people's weather.

team.firenze.summary: Muscle was never the wine-dark house's argument, and the horsepower spring proved it twice. Where the FIRENZE 99 V12 could not answer the new monsters down the straights, F. Elssler answered with cold consistency, banking his arithmetic while the brave machines expired around him. I. Germi kept the tight rounds operatic, the FIRENZE 500 still leaving the garage like silverwork. But the drawing office had done its sums all autumn: if the aria could not out-sing the thunder, the car would have to out-slice the air. In a corner of the wind-shed, a wing was being shaved to almost nothing.
team.firenze.objective: Bank cold arithmetic where the muscle runs out and invent the answer to the power gap.
team.firenze.definingEvent: Elssler's podium arithmetic through the smoke plague: the ice, harvesting the brave men's fires.
team.firenze.carryForward: A shaved wing in the wind-shed and a philosophy being born: the drag winter is coming.

team.millions.summary: The horsepower spring arrived, for the chrome garage, on a transporter stacked with fresh engines. While the floor rebuilt the same tired unit week after week, the MILLIONS 189 ran new DICK MD V10s the way other teams run new tyres, and N. Jones made sure the whole paddock knew the cost of each one. G. Alberti carried the #6 with a champion's manners and a rising, private distaste for the invoices. The gold bought what gold buys: finishes, mostly. The smoke plague took its tax even from the rich, some Sundays, and the garage paid the surcharge without blinking.
team.millions.objective: Outspend the plague: fresh power every Sunday, whatever the ledger says.
team.millions.definingEvent: The engine allocation ledger nobody else on the grid could have signed.
team.millions.carryForward: A patrician driver's distaste for the freight, and a garage learning that even gold pays the smoke tax.

team.bestowal.summary: At last, weather made for German steel. The Gen-4 spring was the formula the given house had ordered three winters earlier, and the BESTOWAL 167's sealed VAPOR DN was suddenly the benchmark down every straight that mattered, rival engineers staring at its telemetry trace like scripture. M. Blume planted his foot down West Germany's forest blasts, the cathedral run the season's spiritual centre, the timing beam checked twice. A. Picos converted Sundays with warm precision. The smoke plague taxed the steel like everyone, the treeline eating a season's engines whole, but the house paid its tithe and howled on.
team.bestowal.objective: Make the power cathedrals Bestowal country and turn the new formula into the house's own weather.
team.bestowal.definingEvent: Blume's cathedral run down the forest blasts, the timing beam checked twice.
team.bestowal.carryForward: The cathedral has a guardian, the crown has been warned, and the smoke is priced as a tithe.

team.blanche.summary: The smoke plague read, to the whitecoats, like an audit of everyone else's housekeeping. The BLANCHE 582 was never going to out-muscle the new monsters, so the garage did what it does: logged every bolt, ran every session, and finished while the thunder expired in the treeline. J. Herbin's metronome ticked at the front of LEVEL B through the loudest spring in memory, P. White believed through the noise, and the DELTA 103 V10's honesty looked less like a compromise every Sunday. The door to LEVEL A stayed shut. The knocking, however, now came with a finishing record attached.
team.blanche.objective: Finish everything the monsters cannot and make the door argument with a complete car.
team.blanche.definingEvent: A spotless finishing run through the plague, the white car still there when the smoke cleared.
team.blanche.carryForward: The knock now carries a ledger, and the clean garage's arithmetic improves every Sunday.

team.tyrant.summary: Speed, the boot camp holds, is only courage with better funding, and the horsepower spring did not change the creed. The TYRANT 548's brakes, already the cruellest in LEVEL B, mattered more now that everyone arrived at the corners faster than the asphalt liked, and G. Gould turned the new terminal speeds into a longer dare. M. Hamano's crossover ice kept the arithmetic honest. The LIZZIE 24 V8 was leaned on harder than ever and complained in the usual currency. Upstairs, the monsters dueled; down here, the fists simply chose longer braking zones.
team.tyrant.objective: Turn the new terminal speeds into longer dares and keep LEVEL B afraid of the braking zone.
team.tyrant.definingEvent: Gould's braking zones, extended by the horsepower era, occupied without negotiation.
team.tyrant.carryForward: The fists need a longer reach, and the boot camp spends the winter building brave machinery.

team.losel.summary: The grey graphs of winter became the grey reality of spring. The data room had priced Gen-4 honestly, allocations, rebuild cycles, the cost of fury, and the LOSEL 125 lived inside those sums all season, E. Pacheco's cold equations banking what the plague left on the table, W. Dehehe's blue needle steady. The house's one hedge paid exactly as filed: the VAPOR DNPQ V8 could be opened by its own mechanics, so while richer garages queued for fresh power, Losel rebuilt in-house and kept running. The winter report's conclusion was dry: the model cannot win yet, but the model survives.
team.losel.objective: Live inside the sums, rebuild in-house, and let the plague prove the spreadsheet.
team.losel.definingEvent: The rebuilt-in-house engine finishing where the fresh ones expired.
team.losel.carryForward: An allocation spreadsheet the whole paddock would kill for, and a model that knows it survives.

team.may.summary: They nearly did not make the flag. The Gen-4 winter priced the little green car out of everything, and May arrived at San Marino having sold anything not bolted down to afford a starting allocation of engines. Then the spring came, the LORRY 32 V8 outlasted the monsters, and G. Turner went back to waving, only now the whole floor was marching behind the leaf-green banner, because the smallest garage on the grid had stared down the dearest formula in history and shown up anyway. The MAY 555 bloomed on the warm afternoons. Survival, it turned out, photographed beautifully.
team.may.objective: Make the grid by any means, then make the little teams believe it can be done.
team.may.definingEvent: San Marino: the entry scraped together, the banner planted, the flag taken.
team.may.carryForward: The floor marches behind the green car now, and hope has become a form of leadership.

team.bullets.summary: Smoke, cough, arrive: the insurgency's whole season in three acts. The home-built Gen-4 thunder was asked for fury it was never billed for, and the LIZZIE 24 V8 answered with the paddock's favourite underdog story, the featherweight BULLETS 560 smoking through Fridays, coughing through Saturdays, and arriving in the argument on Sundays anyway. G. Ceara's fire only grew with the odds, the grandees' mirrors still carrying the #17 on the afternoons that mattered. The establishment's engineers, watching the lock-up weld its own power together, began saying a dangerous sentence: imagine that chassis with a real engine.
team.bullets.objective: Keep the home-built thunder arriving and grow the legend on exactly the longer odds.
team.bullets.definingEvent: The three-act season: smoke on Friday, cough on Saturday, arrival on Sunday.
team.bullets.carryForward: The paddock has said the dangerous sentence aloud, and the lock-up has heard it.

team.dardan.summary: The front pages belonged to the sister car's smoke, and the clan read every one of them like a bill coming due. The DARDAN 700 ran the horsepower spring the Dardan way, oxblood over hammered bronze, sharpened for the walled rounds, K. Alfven banking with ice-over-oxblood hands while E. Bellini's temper shortened another notch. The VAPOR DNPQ V8 gave its coarse, steady torque and its documented honesty, virtues the era had stopped applauding. The truce that was never declared held, technically. The silence from the oxblood garage did not sound like peace. It sounded like saving.
team.dardan.objective: Bank through the plague, keep the walled rounds bloody, and save the grudge for its day.
team.dardan.definingEvent: The sister car's smoke on every front page, filed in the clan's ledger under debts.
team.dardan.carryForward: A temper one notch from the edge, and a grudge compounding like interest.

team.linden.summary: The harvest came in on smoke and patience. The LINDEN LN198 finished the plague season the way it finishes everything, M. Moreau's hands and the LORRY 32 V8's seasoned torque outlasting monsters that cost ten times the entry fee. The old root's season was lived as much in the briefing room as on the road: the pace-car whispers from the wet years grew louder with every expired engine, and Moreau asked his question at every drivers' meeting, were the circuits ready for these speeds, and every meeting adjourned without an answer. The tree kept the minutes. The tree keeps everything.
team.linden.objective: Harvest the plague and make the briefing room hear the question it keeps dodging.
team.linden.definingEvent: The question asked at every drivers' meeting, answered at none.
team.linden.carryForward: The whispers have a voice now, and the voice is the oldest number on the grid.

team.minarae.summary: The treeline ate a season's worth of engines whole; Minarae ate hairpins. The horsepower spring shrank the canyon argument everywhere except in the concrete canyons themselves, where B. Miller went brake-late past expiring monsters in the plainest car on the grid. J. Nono learned more in one savage year than three gentle ones, the SEGA SG1000 V8 again proving itself the best first teacher precisely by refusing to teach thunder. The scouts' notebooks filled with a new observation: the on-ramp's graduates arrive already knowing how to finish. In the power era, that is a premium skill.
team.minarae.objective: Turn the plague into a teaching year and make finishing the on-ramp's premium export.
team.minarae.definingEvent: Miller brake-late past an expiring monster: the canyon craft outlasting the cathedral power.
team.minarae.carryForward: Two graduates priced upward by the plague, and a reputation for teaching the unfashionable virtues.

team.rigel.summary: Monsters die; ghosts endure. The horsepower spring filled the treeline with expired aristocrats, and the RIGEL 3000, beautiful, brittle, and gloriously old-fashioned, circulated through the carnage with the serenity of a house that has been buried before and knows the procedure. One Sunday the midnight-blue car came home ahead of three smoking grandees, and the museum opened the good bottle, updated the ledger in calligraphic hand, and allowed itself an evening of looking forward. The LORRY 32 V8's superstitions about finishing had never been so fashionable. The star's light, the lifers noted, is simply slower to arrive.
team.rigel.objective: Outlast the monsters and add one plague-year festival to the ledger.
team.rigel.definingEvent: The Sunday the ghost finished ahead of three expired aristocrats.
team.rigel.carryForward: The museum has learned the new era's mercy: fury is mortal, patience is not.

team.comet.summary: Suddenly every garage on the grid had a plume, and the romantics of the floor felt almost fashionable. The smoke plague levelled what the horsepower had unlevelled, and E. Tornio's silent right foot produced two Saturday flyers and, the season's small miracle, a Sunday finish with the LIZZIE 24 V8 still singing at the flag. The COMET 323's own reliability account came due on schedule, of course; some traditions are load-bearing. The quarrel with Cool over whose decline was grander paused briefly so both crews could compare other people's smoke. Hope is a maintenance schedule. This year, the schedule held.
team.comet.objective: Use the levelling plague to burn bright and, for once, still be burning at the flag.
team.comet.definingEvent: The small miracle: a flyer on Saturday and the same engine singing on Sunday evening.
team.comet.carryForward: The schedule held, the reel grew, and the comet men have stopped apologising for hoping.

team.orchis.summary: The hottest, angriest summer in memory was no country for a glasshouse flower. The MISFIRE 50 V8 drank its cooling and sulked through the heat, the ORCHIS 056 wilting on the scorched afternoons as the ledger feared. But C. Tegner's deeper science, the hour, the wind off the grandstands, the cloud the hired professionals missed, found the season's few cool windows, and twice the pale car bloomed through them into points that embarrassed the big garages. The greenhouse's new edge held. It simply had few afternoons to work with, and the frost gambler spent autumn praying for a colder calendar.
team.orchis.objective: Find the few cool windows the scorched season offers and bloom through all of them.
team.orchis.definingEvent: Two cool-window blooms in the hottest summer, stolen on sky-reading alone.
team.orchis.carryForward: A science that works and a calendar that will not cooperate: the greenhouse prays for cold.

team.zeroforce.summary: The weekly trial now came with a price list. The Gen-4 winter had itemised survival, engines, allocations, rebuilds, and the ZEROFORCE 231 spent the season at the wrong end of every column, P. Klinger nursing the same tired LORRY 32 V8 while the gold garages ran fresh power like water. Yet the plague is the floor's oldest ally: when the monsters expired, the battered car inherited places without precedent, and not being last at the flag became, twice, a festival with actual champagne, cheap bottle opened anyway. The crew's verdict on the horsepower era remains unprintable. The lights stayed on.
team.zeroforce.objective: Nurse the one tired engine, inherit what the plague drops, and keep the lights on.
team.zeroforce.definingEvent: Two not-last festivals in the plague year, celebrated with the cheap bottle.
team.zeroforce.carryForward: The gap between fresh power and rebuilt power, measured weekly, in chalk, on the wall.

team.joke.summary: The last coin bought the opening flag. The JOKE 777 nearly missed San Marino entirely, the Gen-4 ledger demanding numbers the carnival had never possessed, and L. Dufay arrived with a starting allocation, a full tank of cheek, and nothing else. Then the monsters began expiring, and the POND V8, impossible to hurry, impossible to kill, became the punchline that outlasted the joke's richer tellers. The sandbagging continued on Saturdays, for tradition; the Sunday charges arrived on schedule. The establishment, which had spent the winter pricing the future, did not enjoy being out-finished by a yacht.
team.joke.objective: Make the flag on the last coin and outlast the machines that cost the earth.
team.joke.definingEvent: San Marino reached on the final coin, then the monsters out-finished by a yacht.
team.joke.carryForward: The carnival survives the dearest winter, and the establishment still cannot file the #16.

team.lares.summary: The retirement column ran long all year, and the hearth read it like scripture. The RAM V12 beneath the stout LARES 92 ran on geological principles the horsepower spring never consulted, and P. Arai's gentleman's patience turned the smoke plague into the second great harvest of his career: no heroics, no raised voice, just the red car circulating at the flag while the thunder wrote itself off in the treeline. The finishing record grew past another round number nobody else was counting. The lamp stayed lit, the door stayed open, and the floor's frightened garages kept finding both.
team.lares.objective: Harvest the plague with patience and prove the creed against the loudest year yet.
team.lares.definingEvent: The second great harvest: the red car outlasting the thunder all spring.
team.lares.carryForward: The record rounds another number, and the hearth's creed starts sounding like prophecy.

team.feet.summary: Overtime, again. The smoke plague turned every Sunday into the afternoons the grafters train for: slippery with other people's oil, unfair, and priced in effort rather than machinery. J. Rampal dragged the FEET 13 and its hand-fettled, rebuilt YOUGEN V10 past expiring works engines with the regularity of a man punching a clock, because that is exactly what he was doing. Old Boots does not do drama; Old Boots does distances, and in the year the monsters learned to die, the distance paid better than ever. The pit lane's mechanics, who know what things cost, kept count for them.
team.feet.objective: Turn the plague into overtime and drag the rebuilt unit past the fresh ones.
team.feet.definingEvent: Rampal past an expiring works engine, again: the season's most honest photograph.
team.feet.carryForward: The sweat invoice grows, and the men who know what things cost are keeping count.

team.serga.summary: The plague made midnight mechanics of the paddock, and the paddock was still slower than E. Sambena with a torch. The horsepower spring was, to the man who had always rebuilt what others replaced, simply everyone discovering his life: the SERGA 1000 smoked, was mended, arrived, was celebrated, the SC3000 F12 eager once warmed, opinionated about its oil. On the year's worst afternoons the grey car beat machinery worth ten of it. The countdown nobody would say aloud ticked closer beneath the spares shelf, and pride went on pretending the shelf was a pantry.
team.serga.objective: Out-weld the plague and keep the grey car arriving ahead of the aristocrats' scrap.
team.serga.definingEvent: The paddock discovering Sambena's life, one expired engine at a time.
team.serga.carryForward: The countdown closer, the shelf barer, and pride still insisting it is a pantry.

team.cool.summary: From the deckchairs, every cloud on the horizon that spring was an engine. The smoke plague was the calmest garage's favourite kind of season: drama everywhere, none of it theirs. A. Delvaux composed his love letters to the hairpins while the thunder detonated in the treeline, the COOL 05's CORSE V8 sulking in its electrics on schedule and running sweetly off it, which the crew accepted like weather. A point arrived on an afternoon the monsters did not, celebrated at the cheap end like a settled title. The quarrel with Comet now required a smoke appendix.
team.cool.objective: Enjoy the show, accept the weather, and collect what the plague drops.
team.cool.definingEvent: A point on an afternoon the monsters did not finish, toasted at deckchair pace.
team.cool.carryForward: The quarrel with Comet now has a smoke appendix, and the deckchairs remain undefeated.

team.moon.summary: The logbook's plague-year line wrote itself: present when the monsters were not. The RAM V12 at the heart of the MOON 292 does not do fury, and the horsepower spring's constant detonations made continuation look like a strategy, K. Yepes waving at the machines that lapped him and, increasingly, at their smoking remains. The gap to the pack shrank by attrition's courtesy, and the far side noted it in the same low voices it uses for moonrise. The pit wall's moonshots stayed unfired; you do not gamble an irreplaceable car in a demolition season. The telescope never moved.
team.moon.objective: Outlast the monsters, log the courtesy of attrition, and keep the telescope steady.
team.moon.definingEvent: The season's line in the logbook: present when the monsters were not.
team.moon.carryForward: The gap measurably smaller, by the plague's courtesy, and the orbit quietly rising.

team.iris.summary: The violet flame went public in the horsepower spring. B. Salgado stood before the press and committed the white-glove house to chasing the power gap, the vow of two winters past now a programme with a budget, and then went out and drove the commitment, dragging the IRIS 717 into fights the PRISM 90 V10 had no business winning and grinning when it won some anyway. The jewel-cut balance knifed the sweepers as ever; the straights remained the honest gap. The gloves, which had been clenching for two seasons, were now formally, publicly, on the table.
team.iris.objective: Make the power-gap chase official policy and drive it like a debt.
team.iris.definingEvent: The public commitment: the gloves on the table, the programme funded, the press told.
team.iris.carryForward: A campaign with a budget and a champion who drives like a man collecting.

team.azalea.summary: The frost had waited for a season this loud. While the monsters howled down the straights and expired in the treeline, M. Larssen read the smoke plague patiently, and on the year's decisive afternoon, when the thunder had spent itself and the big board was a war memorial, the AZALEA 808 opened over the wreckage, the BLOOM 88 V8 building from held breath to full song exactly when the stage was worth it. The sister war tilted audibly: the loud sister promised thunder all spring, and the cold one collected the afternoon the thunder could not finish.
team.azalea.objective: Wait for the afternoon the thunder cannot finish, and open over it.
team.azalea.definingEvent: The year's decisive afternoon: the bloom over the wreckage, the monsters silent.
team.azalea.carryForward: The family ledger tilts further: thunder promises, frost delivers.
